Task id: T70 (ledgerRefs goals:G5) — ROUND 2 re-dispatch after review.

Branch implement/T70 (HEAD 4bd6e683687a7b28702eddab799f2f7b2b6fa5d2), checked out in worktree /home/pavel/work/safe/fecalia/.claude/worktrees/agent-a5e1fb1cab9a92f66. Work THERE if reachable; else `git switch -c t70-fix implement/T70` in your own worktree via the shared .git store, commit, report the EXACT sha (copy from git output).

Go repo — gate `go build ./... && go vet ./... && go test ./internal/device/... -race && go test ./...` via `nix develop -c`.

Prior-round reviewer criticism to address (2 items):
1. [fable+opus] updateResolution cannot activate from the all-empty boot state: the reconciliation branch (failover.go:248-273) is gated on specIdx == h.activeSpec, and activeSpec == -1 (hostname-only peer, every spec empty at boot) never matches — the first successful resolution populates the expansion but never adopts an active entry and never repoints the bond. check() cannot rescue a single-hostname peer (single-record expansion keeps flatLenLocked()==1, permanently under the total<2 guard). A single-hostname peer's bond therefore never receives any endpoint through this controller — contradicting peerNeedsHubFailover's own rationale and T73's contract (which coordinates solely through this update API and explicitly runs for single-hostname peers). Fix: when activeSpec == -1 and the flattened list becomes non-empty after the swap, adopt the first flattened entry (set activeSpec/activeAddr, one SetPeerRemote + one rehandshake, reset lastSwitch, re-map idx) and add a test for the hostname-only boot → first-resolution path.
2. [fable] The active-IP-change repoint in updateResolution (failover.go:256-264) does not reset h.lastSwitch, violating the field's documented invariant and the settle-dwell rule in docs/design.md: after a repoint under hub loss (T73's liveness-loss flow), the next check() tick still reads all-down and, with the dwell elapsed against the stale lastSwitch, immediately advances off the just-repointed endpoint — a second disruptive SetPeerRemote before the new address can prove itself. Fix: set h.lastSwitch = h.clock.Now() in the repoint branch (and in the adoption path from item 1) and add a test asserting check() takes no action within the dwell after a repoint.

Apply both, re-run the gate, commit, return the structured result json with the exact resultCommit sha.

- baboon: Baboon data model compiler reference. Covers model syntax, multi-version evolution, code generation, wire-format conversions, database migrations, backward compatibility, and version negotiation. TRIGGER when: designing, changing, or discussing any external API, working with .baboon model files, updating data models, adding new fields or types, bumping model versions, running code generation, implementing conversions, writing database migrations, or discussing API versioning strategy.
- constructive-test-taxonomy: Multi-axis classification of tests (Intention × Encapsulation × Isolation) for evaluating test quality and steering test-writing decisions. Replaces fuzzy Unit/Integration/E2E vocabulary with precise tags and a maintenance-cost heuristic. TRIGGER when: writing new tests, reviewing a test suite, deciding what to test or at what level, discussing mocks vs. real dependencies, classifying tests, or arguing about test coverage or test-strategy tradeoffs.
- dual-tests: Pattern for testing code that touches external systems: write one abstract test suite against an interface, then run it against both the production adapter (real DB/API/FS) and a hand-written in-memory dummy. Prefers manual dummies over auto-generated mocks. TRIGGER when: designing code that depends on a database, HTTP API, message queue, filesystem, or other external system; choosing between mocks, fakes, or dummies; adding integration points; setting up test infrastructure; or when the `constructive-test-taxonomy` skill calls for moving Communication-tier tests toward Blackbox-Atomic.
- environment: Bubblewrap "yolo" sandbox escape workflow (exchange scripts for reaching $HOME, system paths, or files outside the sandbox) plus in-sandbox nix/direnv usage. TRIGGER only when $SMIND_SANDBOXED=1 — i.e. running inside the yolo sandbox and you need to reach something outside it. SKIP entirely when $SMIND_SANDBOXED is unset: you are unsandboxed, so write directly and ignore this skill.
- izumi: Best-practice playbook for the izumi Scala ecosystem — distage (DI), distage-framework (roles/RoleAppMain), distage-testkit (test harness with memoization), distage-framework-docker (test containers), logstage (structured logging), and BIO (bifunctor effect typeclasses). Covers idiomatic bindings, Activation/Axis switching, Lifecycle discipline, plugin layout, and how to apply the `dual-tests` pattern inside distage so one test body runs against both a dummy and a real-DB adapter. TRIGGER when: writing or reviewing Scala code that imports `izumi.distage.*`, `izumi.logstage.*`, `izumi.functional.bio.*`, `distage.*`, `logstage.*`; designing a new distage plugin or Role; wiring a testkit `SpecZIO`/`Spec2`/`Spec1`; configuring `ContainerDef`/`DockerSupportModule`; choosing between `StandardAxis.Repo` or `Scene` tags; or implementing the dual-tests pattern in a distage project. The canonical reference project is https://github.com/7mind/distage-example.
- resilient-ws-ui: Guidelines for building applications that maintain reliable WebSocket connections to a backend and surface connection health clearly to the user. Invoke ONLY when the user explicitly references this skill by name (e.g. "/resilient-ws-ui", "use the resilient-ws-ui skill"). Do NOT auto-invoke based on topic match.
- tass: Target/Actual State Separation (TASS) pattern for reliable state management in event-driven control systems. TRIGGER when: implementing device or entity state management, designing control systems, working with target vs actual state separation, or building event-driven systems with commanded/observed state.
- cq:advance: Advance the WHOLE flow one cycle — chain /cq:investigate:advance, /cq:plan:advance, /cq:implement:advance to quiescence, then report DRAINED / BLOCKED-ON-QUESTIONS / MIXED.

Judges ONE task's implementation, splits findings into autonomously-fixable criticism[], user-only questions[], and out-of-scope defects[], and emits the verdict as JSON on stdout. Writes NOTHING to the ledger — the orchestrator records the terminal review.

- codex:gpt-5-4-prompting: Internal guidance for composing Codex and GPT-5.4 prompts for coding, review, diagnosis, and research tasks inside the Codex Claude Code plugin
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.

Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.

Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").

content.content:
1	package device
2	
3	import (
4		"net/netip"
5		"sync"
6		"time"
7	
8		awgdevice "github.com/amnezia-vpn/amneziawg-go/device"
9	
10		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/bind"
11		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/config"
12		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/log"
13		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/telemetry"
14	)
15	
16	// hubFailoverSettle is the dwell a newly-selected concentrator endpoint is given to
17	// prove itself LIVE before the controller may advance again. It exists to keep a
18	// STILL-DOWN reading — caused merely by echoes not having returned from the just-
19	// selected hub yet — from skipping straight past a healthy standby.
20	//
21	// It must comfortably exceed the liveness UP-recovery latency: a DOWN path comes UP
22	// after telemetry.DefaultUpSuccesses (3) probe echoes at telemetry.DefaultProbeInterval
23	// (200ms) ≈ 600ms once probes reach a reachable hub. At 3s the dwell clears that with a
24	// wide margin for jitter/RTT, and also bounds the re-advance cadence while a whole hub
25	// fleet is down to at most one switch (one fresh handshake) per 3s. Hub loss is a
26	// coarse, rare event (a concentrator down), distinct from the sub-second PER-PATH
27	// failover the schedulers own, so a switch latency on this scale is acceptable.
28	const hubFailoverSettle = 3 * time.Second
29	
30	// hubHealth is one path's liveness verdict, the read side of the T13 telemetry state
31	// machine (*telemetry.Prober satisfies it). The hub-failover controller reads exactly
32	// the same up/down verdict the schedulers select on, so "hub loss" is derived from the
33	// very liveness plane per-path failover already runs on — no second detector.
34	type hubHealth interface {
35		State() telemetry.PathState
36	}
37	
38	// peerRemote repoints the whole bond's wire remote at a new concentrator endpoint
39	// (*bind.Multipath satisfies it via SetPeerRemote). It is the action half of a hub
40	// switch on the DATA/PROBE plane; the re-handshake is separate (rehandshake).
41	type peerRemote interface {
42		SetPeerRemote(ap netip.AddrPort)
43	}
44	
45	// rehandshake drops the current WireGuard session and initiates a FRESH one against the
46	// peer's static key — no hub-to-hub state handoff. The device wires it to the engine
47	// peer (expire keypairs + send handshake initiation); a test injects a counter.
48	type rehandshake func()
49	
50	// hubFailover is the edge-side active-standby concentrator failover controller (Q18/T57).
51	// It watches the per-path liveness plane and, when EVERY path to the currently-active
52	// concentrator is DOWN simultaneously (HUB LOSS — distinct from a single path failing,
53	// which the schedulers already absorb), advances to the next endpoint in the ordered
54	// concentrator list, repoints every path's remote at it, and initiates a fresh WG
55	// re-handshake. It re-arms against the new endpoint and, if that one is also fully down,
56	// advances again.
57	//
58	// End-of-list policy: WRAP (round-robin modulo the list length). Once the last standby
59	// is exhausted the controller cycles back to index 0 and keeps retrying every endpoint
60	// in order. Wrap is chosen over stop because it preserves availability — a concentrator
61	// that RECOVERS earlier in the list is retried and settled on within one full cycle,
62	// whereas stopping at the last endpoint would strand the edge on a dead hub even after
63	// endpoint 0 came back. The settle dwell bounds the churn to one switch (one handshake)
64	// per hubFailoverSettle while the whole fleet is down, so the round-robin is a slow,
65	// bounded retry, not a storm.
66	//
67	// GUARD (must-hold invariant): a SINGLE-endpoint list takes NO failover action at all —
68	// check returns immediately, so the remote is never repointed and no re-handshake is
69	// initiated. A one-concentrator deployment therefore behaves EXACTLY as it did before
70	// T57 (the endpoints-list "list" is the one-element normalization of the legacy single
71	// endpoint form, per T54).
72	type hubFailover struct {
73		// specs is the ORDERED concentrator endpoint set, one failoverSpec per configured
74		// endpoint entry (config.Peer.EndpointSpecs), in TOML order. Each spec OWNS its
75		// current expansion ([]netip.AddrPort): a literal is a fixed single entry; a hostname
76		// is its latest resolved record set (empty until the resolver — wired by a later task —
77		// first calls updateResolution). The FLATTENED concatenation of every spec's expansion,
78		// in spec order, is the failover order; the flattened index is what `check` advances.
79		// The set is MUTABLE: updateResolution swaps a spec's expansion in place under mu.
80		specs []failoverSpec
81		// health is the per-path liveness, one entry per configured path. Immutable after
82		// construction (the prober set is fixed for the tunnel's life).
83		health []hubHealth
84	
85		remote      peerRemote
86		rehandshake rehandshake
87		clock       telemetry.Clock
88		// settle is the dwell a freshly-selected endpoint gets before another advance is
89		// allowed (hubFailoverSettle in production; a test injects its own).
90		settle time.Duration
91		log    log.Logger
92	
93		mu sync.Mutex
94		// activeSpec and activeAddr are the SPEC-SCOPED identity of the ACTIVE endpoint (R70):
95		// the pair (owning spec index, AddrPort) — NEVER a bare flattened index and NEVER a
96		// bare AddrPort. Because a hostname may legitimately re-resolve onto the SAME AddrPort
97		// as another spec's literal (T67's load-time duplicate check is textual host:port
98		// only), duplicate AddrPort VALUES may appear across different specs in the flattened
99		// list; a bare-value re-map could silently bind the active pointer to the wrong spec's
100		// entry, so the active endpoint is addressed by (specIdx, AddrPort). activeSpec == -1
101		// means no active entry yet (every spec's expansion is empty). Guarded by mu.
102		activeSpec int
103		activeAddr netip.AddrPort
104		// idx is the ACTIVE endpoint's position in the FLATTENED list, DERIVED from
105		// (activeSpec, activeAddr) and re-mapped after every mutation. It is a read-only cache
106		// for observation/logging; the source of truth is the spec-scoped identity above. -1
107		// when there is no active entry. Guarded by mu.
108		idx int
109		// lastSwitch is when the active endpoint was last (re)selected — initialized to
110		// construction time so endpoint 0 gets the SAME settle grace at boot that a switched
111		// endpoint gets, preventing a boot-time false failover while probers are still DOWN
112		// before the first echo returns. Guarded by mu.
113		lastSwitch time.Time
114	}
115	
116	// failoverSpec is one ordered endpoint entry together with its CURRENT expansion. A
117	// literal entry's expansion is the fixed single AddrPort; a hostname entry's expansion is
118	// its latest resolved record set (Q32 multi-record), swapped in place by updateResolution.
119	// The spec itself (config.EndpointSpec) is retained so the controller can tell a hostname
120	// spec — which may grow/shrink at runtime — from an immutable literal, e.g. when deciding
121	// whether the failover loop may ever have work to do.
122	type failoverSpec struct {
123		spec  config.EndpointSpec
124		addrs []netip.AddrPort
125	}
126	
127	// newHubFailover builds the controller over an ordered list of IP-LITERAL endpoints — the
128	// legacy all-literal form (each address becomes its own single-entry literal spec). It is
129	// a pure constructor (no goroutine, no engine dependency beyond the injected collaborators)
130	// so its decision logic is unit-testable against a fake clock, fake health, and fake
131	// remote/rehandshake.
132	func newHubFailover(endpoints []netip.AddrPort, health []hubHealth, remote peerRemote, rh rehandshake, clock telemetry.Clock, settle time.Duration, lg log.Logger) *hubFailover {
133		specs := make([]failoverSpec, len(endpoints))
134		for i, ap := range endpoints {
135			specs[i] = failoverSpec{spec: config.EndpointSpec{Addr: ap}, addrs: []netip.AddrPort{ap}}
136		}
137		return newHubFailoverFromSpecs(specs, health, remote, rh, clock, settle, lg)
138	}
139	
140	// newHubFailoverFromSpecs builds the controller over the ordered, spec-keyed endpoint set.
141	// The active endpoint is initialized to the FIRST flattened entry (spec order); when every
142	// spec's expansion is empty (e.g. a hostname-only peer before the resolver has run) there
143	// is no active entry yet (activeSpec == -1) and check takes no action until an expansion
144	// appears. Like newHubFailover it injects no goroutine or engine dependency.
145	func newHubFailoverFromSpecs(specs []failoverSpec, health []hubHealth, remote peerRemote, rh rehandshake, clock telemetry.Clock, settle time.Duration, lg log.Logger) *hubFailover {
146		h := &hubFailover{
147			specs:       specs,
148			health:      health,
149			remote:      remote,
150			rehandshake: rh,
151			clock:       clock,
152			settle:      settle,
153			lastSwitch:  clock.Now(),
154			log:         lg,
155		}
156		h.activeSpec, h.activeAddr = h.entryAtLocked(0)
157		h.idx = h.flatIndexLocked(h.activeSpec, h.activeAddr)
158		return h
159	}
160	
161	// flatLenLocked is the number of endpoints in the FLATTENED list (the sum of every spec's
162	// current expansion length). Caller holds mu.
163	func (h *hubFailover) flatLenLocked() int {
164		n := 0
165		for i := range h.specs {
166			n += len(h.specs[i].addrs)
167		}
168		return n
169	}
170	
171	// canFailoverLocked reports whether the controller could EVER take a failover action: the
172	// flattened list already holds >= 2 endpoints, or SOME spec is a hostname whose runtime
173	// resolution may yet grow it. An all-literal set with a single entry can never grow, so its
174	// loop is a pure no-op and need not run. Caller holds mu.
175	func (h *hubFailover) canFailoverLocked() bool {
176		if h.flatLenLocked() >= 2 {
177			return true
178		}
179		for i := range h.specs {
180			if h.specs[i].spec.IsName {
181				return true
182			}
183		}
184		return false
185	}
186	
187	// flatIndexLocked returns the position of (specIdx, addr) in the FLATTENED list, or -1 if
188	// no such entry exists. It matches BOTH the owning spec index AND the AddrPort — never the
189	// bare value — so a duplicate AddrPort contributed by a DIFFERENT spec is skipped (R70);
190	// within the owning spec the FIRST occurrence wins. Caller holds mu.
191	func (h *hubFailover) flatIndexLocked(specIdx int, addr netip.AddrPort) int {
192		flat := 0
193		for si := range h.specs {
194			for _, a := range h.specs[si].addrs {
195				if si == specIdx && a == addr {
196					return flat
197				}
198				flat++
199			}
200		}
201		return -1
202	}
203	
204	// entryAtLocked maps a FLATTENED index back to its (owning spec index, AddrPort), or
205	// (-1, zero) when flatIdx is out of range (including an empty flattened list). Caller holds mu.
206	func (h *hubFailover) entryAtLocked(flatIdx int) (int, netip.AddrPort) {
207		if flatIdx < 0 {
208			return -1, netip.AddrPort{}
209		}
210		n := 0
211		for si := range h.specs {
212			for _, a := range h.specs[si].addrs {
213				if n == flatIdx {
214					return si, a
215				}
216				n++
217			}
218		}
219		return -1, netip.AddrPort{}
220	}

222	// containsAddrPort reports whether addrs holds target (exact AddrPort equality).
223	func containsAddrPort(addrs []netip.AddrPort, target netip.AddrPort) bool {
224		for _, a := range addrs {
225			if a == target {
226				return true
227			}
228		}
229		return false
230	}
231	
232	// updateResolution swaps specIdx's expansion under h.mu — the sole mutation point of the
233	// endpoint set — and reconciles the active pointer WITHIN its owning spec (R70 spec-scoped
234	// identity):
235	//   - A STANDBY-only change (specIdx != activeSpec) NEVER touches the bond: the active
236	//     entry is untouched; only the DERIVED flattened idx is re-mapped (an earlier spec
237	//     growing/shrinking shifts the active entry's flattened position without moving it).
238	//   - When the ACTIVE spec's own expansion changes: if the active AddrPort SURVIVES the
239	//     swap within that spec, the active entry is stable — strictly NO repoint (Q31/D32
240	//     no-op suppression), only the idx re-maps. If the active AddrPort is GONE, the active
241	//     endpoint's IP has genuinely changed: repoint via exactly ONE SetPeerRemote (D32
242	//     disruptive → Rebaseline + rehandshake) and one rehandshake to the new expansion's
243	//     first entry.
244	//
245	// A change that empties the active spec (nothing to point at) leaves the active identity
246	// stale — the flattened list no longer contains it — and the next check on hub loss
247	// advances off it; no repoint is issued to a non-existent endpoint.
248	func (h *hubFailover) updateResolution(specIdx int, addrs []netip.AddrPort) {
249		h.mu.Lock()
250		defer h.mu.Unlock()
251	
252		if specIdx < 0 || specIdx >= len(h.specs) {
253			return
254		}
255		h.specs[specIdx].addrs = addrs
256	
257		if specIdx == h.activeSpec {
258			if !containsAddrPort(addrs, h.activeAddr) && len(addrs) > 0 {
259				// The active endpoint's own spec re-resolved off the current AddrPort: its IP
260				// changed. Repoint the bond once and re-handshake against the new first entry.
261				prev := h.activeAddr
262				h.activeAddr = addrs[0]
263				h.remote.SetPeerRemote(h.activeAddr)
264				if h.rehandshake != nil {
265					h.rehandshake()
266				}
267				h.log.Warn("hub failover: active concentrator endpoint re-resolved; repointed remote and re-handshaked",
268					"spec_index", specIdx, "from_endpoint", prev.String(), "to_endpoint", h.activeAddr.String())
269			}
270			// Survived (or empty expansion): no repoint. idx re-maps below.
271		}
272	
273		h.idx = h.flatIndexLocked(h.activeSpec, h.activeAddr)
274	}
275	
276	// allDownLocked reports HUB LOSS: every path's liveness to the active concentrator is
277	// DOWN simultaneously. An empty health set is NOT hub loss (there is nothing to declare
278	// dead — a bind without the probe transport never drives this). Caller holds mu.
279	func (h *hubFailover) allDownLocked() bool {
280		if len(h.health) == 0 {
281			return false
282		}
283		for _, hp := range h.health {
284			if hp.State() != telemetry.StateDown {
285				return false
286			}
287		}
288		return true
289	}
290	
291	// check is one failover-evaluation step: on confirmed hub loss (and once the active
292	// endpoint's settle dwell has elapsed) it advances to the next endpoint, repoints the
293	// bond's remote, and initiates a fresh re-handshake. It is idempotent and cheap in the
294	// steady state (any path up, or a single-endpoint list) — a length check plus a liveness
295	// sweep — so a periodic loop may call it at the probe cadence.
296	func (h *hubFailover) check() {
297		h.mu.Lock()
298		defer h.mu.Unlock()
299	
300		// GUARD: a FLATTENED list of fewer than two endpoints takes NO failover action — no
301		// advance, no remote repoint, no re-handshake — so a one-concentrator deployment is
302		// byte-for-byte the pre-T57 behaviour, and a hostname peer whose resolution has not yet
303		// yielded a second endpoint waits rather than churning.
304		total := h.flatLenLocked()
305		if total < 2 {
306			return
307		}
308		if !h.allDownLocked() {
309			return
310		}
311		// Settle dwell: give the currently-active endpoint time for its liveness to recover
312		// before advancing, so a not-yet-echoed (transiently DOWN) healthy endpoint is not
313		// skipped. Applies at boot (endpoint 0) and after every switch.
314		now := h.clock.Now()
315		if now.Sub(h.lastSwitch) < h.settle {
316			return
317		}
318	
319		// Advance in FLATTENED order from the active entry's current flattened position. The
320		// position is re-derived from the spec-scoped identity every step, so a standby spec
321		// that grew/shrank since the last advance is walked at its new offset. A missing active
322		// (activeSpec == -1, or its AddrPort no longer present) resumes from the head.
323		prev := h.flatIndexLocked(h.activeSpec, h.activeAddr)
324		var nextIdx int
325		if prev < 0 {
326			nextIdx = 0
327		} else {
328			nextIdx = (prev + 1) % total
329		}
330		nextSpec, next := h.entryAtLocked(nextIdx)
331		h.activeSpec = nextSpec
332		h.activeAddr = next
333		h.idx = nextIdx
334		h.lastSwitch = now
335	
336		// Repoint the DATA/PROBE plane FIRST so probes immediately start re-arming detection
337		// against the new endpoint, then initiate the fresh WG re-handshake toward it.
338		h.remote.SetPeerRemote(next)
339		if h.rehandshake != nil {
340			h.rehandshake()
341		}
342		h.log.Warn("hub failover: all paths to active concentrator down; switched endpoint and re-handshaked",
343			"from_index", prev, "to_index", nextIdx, "to_spec", nextSpec, "to_endpoint", next.String(), "endpoints", total)
344	}
345	
346	// deviceRehandshake returns a rehandshake bound to the engine peer identified by pk: it
347	// EXPIRES the peer's current keypairs (dropping the old hub's session — a fresh session
348	// with NO hub-to-hub state handoff) and sends a fresh handshake initiation, which the
349	// Bind fans out to the just-repointed standby endpoint. All hubs in the ordered set
350	// share the peer's single WireGuard static key, so the SAME peer identity re-handshakes
351	// against whichever hub is now active. A peer the engine cannot resolve (never configured
352	// — impossible for the edge's sole peer) yields a no-op so the loop never dereferences
353	// nil. This is the ONLY engine-peer coupling the failover path takes; it stays in the
354	// device package alongside the rest of the engine wiring.
355	func deviceRehandshake(dev *awgdevice.Device, pk config.Key) rehandshake {
356		key := awgdevice.NoisePublicKey(pk.Bytes())
357		return func() {
358			peer := dev.LookupPeer(key)
359			if peer == nil {
360				return
361			}
362			// Drop the current session so the re-handshake starts a FRESH keypair (no hub-to-hub
363			// handoff); ExpireCurrentKeypairs also backdates lastSentHandshake so the immediately
364			// following initiation is not suppressed by the RekeyTimeout guard.
365			peer.ExpireCurrentKeypairs()
366			_ = peer.SendHandshakeInitiation(false)
367		}
368	}
369	
370	// peerNeedsHubFailover reports whether a peer's endpoint set warrants a hub-failover
371	// controller: it does when the peer carries ANY hostname spec (its runtime resolution may
372	// yield further endpoints, and even a single hostname can re-resolve onto a new IP the bond
373	// must be repointed at) OR when >= 2 IP-literal endpoints are configured (an ordered
374	// active-standby list). A single-IP-literal peer needs NO controller — it is byte-for-byte
375	// the pre-G5 single-concentrator deployment (Q29), so the failover path never constructs
376	// or runs for it.
377	func peerNeedsHubFailover(peer config.Peer) bool {
378		for _, s := range peer.EndpointSpecs {
379			if s.IsName {
380				return true
381			}
382		}
383		return len(peer.Endpoints) >= 2
384	}
385	
386	// startHubFailover builds and starts the edge-side hub-failover monitor for cfg's
387	// concentrator peer, or returns a NO-OP stopper when hub failover does not apply: the
388	// concentrator role (it roams edges dynamically and has no endpoint list), a bind without
389	// the probe transport (no probers → no liveness plane), or no peer whose endpoint set
390	// warrants a controller (peerNeedsHubFailover — a single-IP-literal deployment gets none,
391	// staying behaviour-identical to pre-G5). The edge bonds every path to a SINGLE
392	// concentrator, so the whole per-path prober set IS the liveness of the paths to that
393	// concentrator (hub loss = every one DOWN); the controller drives the first qualifying
394	// peer. The returned stopper is what device.Close invokes to halt the loop.
395	func startHubFailover(cfg *config.Config, mp *bind.Multipath, probers []*telemetry.Prober, dev *awgdevice.Device, lg log.Logger) func() {
396		if cfg.Role != config.RoleEdge || len(probers) == 0 {
397			return func() {}
398		}
399		for _, peer := range cfg.WireGuard.Peers {
400			if !peerNeedsHubFailover(peer) {
401				continue
402			}
403			health := make([]hubHealth, len(probers))
404			for i, pr := range probers {
405				health[i] = pr
406			}
407			// Seed each spec's expansion: a literal is its fixed single AddrPort; a hostname
408			// starts EMPTY — resolution is a later task's job (Q30), not wired here.
409			specs := make([]failoverSpec, len(peer.EndpointSpecs))
410			for i, s := range peer.EndpointSpecs {
411				var addrs []netip.AddrPort
412				if !s.IsName {
413					addrs = []netip.AddrPort{s.Addr}
414				}
415				specs[i] = failoverSpec{spec: s, addrs: addrs}
416			}
417			ctrl := newHubFailoverFromSpecs(
418				specs, health, mp,
419				deviceRehandshake(dev, peer.PublicKey),
420				telemetry.SystemClock{}, hubFailoverSettle,
421				lg.Component("hubfailover"),
422			)
423			// Poll at the probe cadence: check is cheap (a length check plus a liveness sweep)
424			// and the settle dwell bounds actual switches, so a responsive poll only tightens
425			// detection latency without churning the remote.
426			return ctrl.startHubFailoverLoop(telemetry.DefaultProbeInterval)
427		}
428		return func() {}
429	}
430	
431	// startHubFailoverLoop launches the failover-evaluation goroutine: it calls check every
432	// interval until the returned stopper is invoked (idempotent). It mirrors the bind's
433	// StartProbeLoop/StartReconcileLoop lifecycle glue — a wall-clock ticker driving a
434	// decision function whose every timing choice runs through the injected Clock, so a test
435	// drives check directly against a fake clock and never starts this goroutine. It is a
436	// no-op (no-op stopper) for a controller that can NEVER fail over (canFailoverLocked: an
437	// all-literal set with fewer than two entries — no hostname to grow it) or a non-positive
438	// interval. A hostname peer whose expansion is still empty DOES start the loop: its
439	// resolution may later populate a second endpoint, and check no-ops cheaply until then.
440	func (h *hubFailover) startHubFailoverLoop(interval time.Duration) (stop func()) {
441		h.mu.Lock()
442		can := h.canFailoverLocked()
443		h.mu.Unlock()
444		if !can || interval <= 0 {
445			return func() {}
446		}
447		done := make(chan struct{})
448		go func() {
449			ticker := time.NewTicker(interval)
450			defer ticker.Stop()
451			for {
452				select {
453				case <-done:
454					return
455				case <-ticker.C:
456					h.check()
457				}
458			}
459		}()
460		var once sync.Once
461		return func() { once.Do(func() { close(done) }) }
462	}

1. **Boot adoption gap**: `updateResolution` only reconciles when `specIdx == h.activeSpec`. content.content:
237	The controller (`hubFailover`) runs a device-lifecycle monitor loop (started after
238	`dev.Up`, stopped before `dev.Close`, alongside the probe/reconcile loops):
239	
240	1. **Detect** hub loss off the **existing per-path liveness plane** — the same
241	   `telemetry.Prober` `State()` the schedulers select on — as *every* path
242	   reporting `StateDown`. No second detector.
243	2. **Advance** to the next endpoint in the ordered list and **repoint every
244	   path's remote** at it via `bind.Multipath.SetPeerRemote` (a uniform override —
245	   a hub switch retargets the whole bond; it supersedes any per-path `dest_addr`).
246	   This changes only the per-path fan-out *beneath* the engine's single virtual
247	   endpoint — **invariant A1 holds** (the engine still sees one peer, no
248	   per-packet endpoint churn).
249	3. **Re-handshake**: expire the peer's current keypairs (a **fresh** session —
250	   **no hub-to-hub state handoff**) and send a fresh handshake initiation toward
251	   the just-repointed standby. This is the only engine-*peer* coupling the
252	   failover path takes; it lives in `internal/device` next to the rest of the
253	   engine wiring (the `conn`-seam isolation of `bind.go` is unaffected).
254	4. **Re-baseline the receive resequencer**: the standby is a *separate process*
255	   whose outer sequence restarts near 1 — far below the release point the prior
256	   hub's high-rate stream advanced the shared `reseq.Resequencer` to. Its first
257	   frame (the WG handshake *response*) would otherwise land in the resequencer's
258	   *suspect* branch and be dropped, because the unauthenticated-DATA resync guard
259	   needs several corroborating low seqs and a freshly re-handshaking standby emits
260	   only ~one DATA frame per `RekeyTimeout` — so corroboration falls outside the
261	   failover window and the tunnel never re-establishes. `SetPeerRemote` therefore
262	   calls `Resequencer.Rebaseline`: because a hub switch is a **trusted control
263	   event** (not a forgeable wire frame), the release point is re-anchored to the
264	   standby's *first* frame immediately, discarding the dead hub's buffered frames
265	   while leaving already-delivered frames untouched.
266	5. **Re-arm** against the new endpoint: probes now flow to it, so if it too is
267	   fully down the controller advances again.
268	
269	**Settle dwell.** After a switch (and at boot for endpoint 0) the newly-selected
270	endpoint gets a fixed dwell (`hubFailoverSettle`, 3 s) to prove itself LIVE before
271	another advance is allowed. It comfortably exceeds the liveness UP-recovery latency
272	(~3 echoes × 200 ms ≈ 600 ms once probes reach a reachable hub), so a still-DOWN
273	reading caused merely by echoes not having returned yet cannot skip past a healthy
274	standby, and it bounds the re-advance cadence (one switch / one handshake per dwell)
275	while a whole hub fleet is down.
276	
277	**End-of-list policy: WRAP** (round-robin modulo the list length). Once the last
278	standby is exhausted the controller cycles back to index 0 and keeps retrying every
279	endpoint in order. Wrap is chosen over *stop* to preserve availability — a hub that
280	recovers earlier in the list is retried and settled on within one cycle, whereas
281	stopping at the last endpoint would strand the edge on a dead hub even after
282	endpoint 0 came back. The settle dwell keeps the round-robin a slow, bounded retry,
283	not a storm.
284	
285	**GUARD (must-hold invariant).** A **single-endpoint** list takes **no** failover
286	action — no advance, no remote repoint, no re-handshake. A one-concentrator
287	deployment (including the legacy single `endpoint` form, normalized to a
288	one-element list) is therefore byte-for-byte the pre-T57 behaviour. The switch and
289	this guard are validated by the real-network netns e2e (`TestHubFailoverStandbySwitch`
290	+ `TestHubFailoverSingleEndpointGuard`, T62) and, over the real internet, by the
291	realhosts mid-transfer WAN-kill tier (`TestRealMidTransferWANKill`, T63).

The controller (`hubFailover`) runs a device-lifecycle monitor loop (started after
`dev.Up`, stopped before `dev.Close`, alongside the probe/reconcile loops):

1. **Detect** hub loss off the **existing per-path liveness plane** — the same
   `telemetry.Prober` `State()` the schedulers select on — as *every* path
   reporting `StateDown`. No second detector.
2. **Advance** to the next endpoint in the ordered list and **repoint every
   path's remote** at it via `bind.Multipath.SetPeerRemote` (a uniform override —
   a hub switch retargets the whole bond; it supersedes any per-path `dest_addr`).
   This changes only the per-path fan-out *beneath* the engine's single virtual
   endpoint — **invariant A1 holds** (the engine still sees one peer, no
   per-packet endpoint churn).
3. **Re-handshake**: expire the peer's current keypairs (a **fresh** session —
   **no hub-to-hub state handoff**) and send a fresh handshake initiation toward
   the just-repointed standby. This is the only engine-*peer* coupling the
   failover path takes; it lives in `internal/device` next to the rest of the
   engine wiring (the `conn`-seam isolation of `bind.go` is unaffected).
4. **Re-baseline the receive resequencer**: the standby is a *separate process*
   whose outer sequence restarts near 1 — far below the release point the prior
   hub's high-rate stream advanced the shared `reseq.Resequencer` to. Its first
   frame (the WG handshake *response*) would otherwise land in the resequencer's
   *suspect* branch and be dropped, because the unauthenticated-DATA resync guard
   needs several corroborating low seqs and a freshly re-handshaking standby emits
   only ~one DATA frame per `RekeyTimeout` — so corroboration falls outside the
   failover window and the tunnel never re-establishes. `SetPeerRemote` therefore
   calls `Resequencer.Rebaseline`: because a hub switch is a **trusted control
   event** (not a forgeable wire frame), the release point is re-anchored to the
   standby's *first* frame immediately, discarding the dead hub's buffered frames
   while leaving already-delivered frames untouched.
5. **Re-arm** against the new endpoint: probes now flow to it, so if it too is
   fully down the controller advances again.

**Settle dwell.** After a switch (and at boot for endpoint 0) the newly-selected
endpoint gets a fixed dwell (`hubFailoverSettle`, 3 s) to prove itself LIVE before
another advance is allowed. It comfortably exceeds the liveness UP-recovery latency
(~3 echoes × 200 ms ≈ 600 ms once probes reach a reachable hub), so a still-DOWN
reading caused merely by echoes not having returned yet cannot skip past a healthy
standby, and it bounds the re-advance cadence (one switch / one handshake per dwell)
while a whole hub fleet is down.

**End-of-list policy: WRAP** (round-robin modulo the list length). Once the last
standby is exhausted the controller cycles back to index 0 and keeps retrying every
endpoint in order. Wrap is chosen over *stop* to preserve availability — a hub that
recovers earlier in the list is retried and settled on within one cycle, whereas
stopping at the last endpoint would strand the edge on a dead hub even after
endpoint 0 came back. The settle dwell keeps the round-robin a slow, bounded retry,
not a storm.

**GUARD (must-hold invariant).** A **single-endpoint** list takes **no** failover
action — no advance, no remote repoint, no re-handshake. A one-concentrator
deployment (including the legacy single `endpoint` form, normalized to a
one-element list) is therefore byte-for-byte the pre-T57 behaviour. The switch and
this guard are validated by the real-network netns e2e (`TestHubFailoverStandbySwitch`
+ `TestHubFailoverSingleEndpointGuard`, T62) and, over the real internet, by the
realhosts mid-transfer WAN-kill tier (`TestRealMidTransferWANKill`, T63).

content.text: Design.md documents the failover controller at a level that does not enumerate `updateResolution`'s internal cases, and the hostname-resolution wiring itself is deferred to a later task (per the code's own "resolution is a later task's job (Q30)"). My fix reinforces the already-documented settle-dwell rule rather than changing user-facing behavior, so no doc change is warranted for these two correctness fixes. Let me commit.

input.command:
git add -A && git commit -q -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
fix(device): adopt first resolution from empty boot + re-arm dwell on repoint (T70 R2)

Two review-round-2 defects in hubFailover.updateResolution:

1. Boot adoption gap. The reconciliation branch was gated on
   specIdx == activeSpec, but a hostname-only peer boots with every spec
   empty and activeSpec == -1, which never matched: the first successful
   resolution populated the expansion yet never adopted an active entry nor
   repointed the bond. check() could not rescue it either — a single-record
   expansion keeps flatLenLocked()==1, permanently under check's total<2
   guard — so a single-hostname peer's bond never received any endpoint.
   Fix: when activeSpec == -1 and the flattened list becomes non-empty,
   adopt its head (set activeSpec/activeAddr, one SetPeerRemote + one
   rehandshake, arm the dwell, re-map idx).

2. Dwell not re-armed on active-IP-change repoint. The repoint branch did
   not reset lastSwitch, so with the dwell already elapsed the next all-down
   check() immediately advanced off the just-repointed endpoint — a second
   disruptive SetPeerRemote before the new address could prove itself. Fix:
   set lastSwitch = clock.Now() in the repoint branch (and the adoption path).

Adds TestHubFailoverBootAdoptsFirstResolution and
TestHubFailoverRepointResetsSettleDwell; both fail on the pre-fix code for
the exact reasons above. Full gate green (build, vet, device -race, all).

Co-Authored-By: Claude Fable 5 <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)" && git rev-parse HEAD
